  1. Once the $batch request is received, SAP Gateway identifies the changeset and starts processing it as a single transaction (LUW).
  2. The framework then reads each operation inside the changeset and calls the respective method.
  3. All operations inside the changeset are executed sequentially (one by one) but within the same transaction context.
  4. The backend logic performs all related table deletions, but no database commit happens immediately during execution.
  5. After all operations are successfully completed, COMMIT WORK is triggered automatically; if any operation fails, the entire changeset is rolled back, ensuring no partial data is saved.

entry.title: Time Dependency in SAP Business Partner: Maintenance and Data Distribution to Non-Time-Dependent App
entry.content:
Time Dependency in Business Partner

Introduction 

In SAP Business Partner, time dependency is a powerful concept that allows master data to be maintained with validity periods. This helps organizations manage historical, current, and future data effectively, with changes applied automatically based on dates. 

While this improves data accuracy and auditability, it also introduces complexity, especially when integrating with systems that do not support time-dependent data 

 
Evolution of Time Dependency 
 
SAP Release 6.40 – Partial Time Dependency 

In SAP Release 6.40, time dependency was partially introduced in SAP Business Partner for specific components, allowing limited use of validity periods in Business Partner data. 

Time dependency was enabled for key areas such as 

  • Bank details (BUT0BK) 
  • Business partner roles (BUT100) 
  • Addresses (BUT020) 
  • Communication data (ADR2 to ADR13, ADRU) 
  • Address usage with full time dependency (BUT021_FS) 

However, since only selected tables supported validity intervals, the concept of time dependency was not uniformly applied across all Business Partner data, resulting in partial time-sliced data management. 

 

SAP Release 7.0 – Full Time Dependency 

In SAP Release 7.0, time dependency was significantly enhanced to provide a more comprehensive and consistent approach to data management in SAP Business Partner. 

Full time dependency was introduced for central data through table BUT000_TD, ensuring that even core Business Partner attributes could be maintained with validity periods. 

Additionally, EEW (Enhancement Framework) extensions were also enabled with time dependency, allowing custom fields to follow the same time-based behavior. 

With these enhancements, SAP achieved complete time-sliced Business Partner data management, where all relevant data can be maintained, tracked, and processed based on validity periods across the entire Business Partner model. 

Steps: 
 
Now let us understand how time dependency is maintained and how data is distributed to non-time-dependent applications. 
 
Time dependency is controlled via customizing table V_TB056, where it can be activated for: 

  • Central Data (BUT000)  
  • Addresses  
  • Bank Details  
  • Roles 

Note: If time dependency is inactive for any dataset (e.g., Address, Bank, Roles), then time slices cannot be maintained for that dataset. 

Time Dependency on Central Data (BUT000)
 
When time dependency is active for central data (Table BUT000), the field validity period automatically appeared during creation or updating with default validity from 01.01.0001 to 31.12.9999. 
 

 
let us create a new validity for the same BP that is from 01.04.2026 to 31.12.9999 and add Search term as SAP, the system automatically splits the existing record as below: 

  • 01.01.0001 → 31.03.2026  
  • 01.04.2026 → 31.12.9999 

Similarly, when future validity is added (e.g., 01.01.2027 → 31.12.2027), the system automatically adjusts intervals without gaps: 

  • 01.01.0001 → 31.03.2026  
  • 01.04.2026 → 31.12.2026  
  • 01.01.2027 → 31.12.2027  
  • 01.01.2028 → 31.12.9999 

 
This is how consistency has maintained for the business Partner Central data when Time dependency is activated for BUT000. 

Now, we will see how this time slices will store in the table when Time dependency is active for the Central data (Table BUT000).  

The currently valid time slice will be stored in the BUT000 table, and the past/future time slice will be stored in the BUT000_TD table. 
 
BUT000 table 

 

BUT000_TD 

Note: Time dependency has been disabled at the header level of BUT000 in SAP S/4HANA and SAP strongly recommends that the end user should NOT maintain the header level data using Customizing to ensure that there are no inconsistencies in the master data. Refer to SAP Note 2379157. 

Now, let us look at other Business Partner datasets such as Addresses, Bank Details, and Roles. 
 
Note: It is important to understand that the concept of time dependency is different for central data (BUT000) compared to other datasets like BUT020, BUT0BK, and BUT100.  

  • For central data (BUT000), the currently valid time slice is stored in the BUT000 table. The past and future time slices for central data are stored separately in the BUT000_TD table. 
  • In contrast, for other datasets such as Addresses, Bank Details, and Roles, all validity records are stored in the same respective table. 

 
Time Dependency for the BP Addresses 

Time dependency for Business Partner addresses allows you to maintain validity periods when it is activated. Once time dependency is active for addresses, each address record can be maintained with a specific valid-from and valid-to date. 
 
When an address is created, the system by default assigns the validity period from the date of creation to end date (31.12.9999), as shown in the screenshot below. 

If a new address is created for the same Business Partner with a different validity period from 07.04.2026 to 31.12.9999 and the previous one changes to 06.04.2026 to 06.04.2026 as shown in the below screen shot. 

As a result, the Business Partner will have multiple address records with different validity periods, and the system determines the Standard address based on the current validity.  
 
Note: The standard address is the one that is automatically picked by applications whenever address data is required for the Business partner. 
 
Note: It is important to note that a Business Partner must always have at least one valid standard address at any given time. 
 
Also, unlike central data (BUT000), all address validity records are stored in the same table as BUT020, where past, current, and future records coexist without any separate time-dependent table. 



Time Dependency for the BP Bank Details 

Time dependency for Business Partner bank details works similarly to addresses. where the validity period is by default maintained from the date of creation (current date) to the maximum end date (31.12.9999). However, this validity period can be adjusted based on business requirements. 
 

Multiple bank details can be maintained for the same Business Partner with different validity periods, as shown in the screenshot below, allowing effective management of time-dependent banking information. 

All such bank detail records, including past, current, and future validities, are stored in the Business Partner bank details table BUT0BK. 

Time Dependency for the BP Roles 

You can create a BP in one or more BP roles. A BP can take part in different business processes simultaneously. For this reason, a BP can have various roles, such as Customer, Supplier, Employee, and so on. 
 
When time dependency is active for roles, each role is maintained with a specific validity period, during which it remains active. Once the validity period expires, the role is automatically deactivated. 

By default, the validity period for a role is maintained from the date of creation (current date) to the maximum end date (31.12.9999), like addresses and bank details, although it can be adjusted based on business requirements. 

You can observe in the below Snap that I have created a vendor role, and its validity is from date of creation [06.04.2026] to end date [31.12.9999]. 

As shown in the screenshot below, a vendor role has been created with validity from 06.04.2026[current date] to 31.12.9999[End-date]. 

In addition, another role such as Disposer has been assigned to the same Business Partner with a different validity period from 06.04.2026 to 31.12.2027. 
 

All such role assignments along with their respective validity periods are stored in the Business Partner roles table BUT100, where multiple role records can coexist for the same Business Partner. 

Distribution of time-dependent business partner data 
 
To understand the distribution of time-dependent Business Partner data, let us consider a Business Partner as shown in the screenshot below, created as a Vendor with two bank details. Bank ID 001 is valid from 31.03.2026 to 14.04.2026, and Bank ID 002 is valid from 15.04.2026 to 31.12.9999. Based on the current date, Bank ID 001 is considered the currently valid record. 

When we check the same vendor in transaction XK03 [non-time dependent application], we can observe that there are no explicit valid-from and valid-to fields displayed. Instead, the system automatically picks and displays the bank detail corresponding to the current validity period, which in this case is Bank ID 001. 

 

How does the system determine the correct current validity record for a Business Partner in non-time-dependent applications? 
 
The system determines the correct current validity record using the report BUPTDTRANSMIT. 
 
How does this report work? 

The report BUPTDTRANSMIT is triggered via the technical job SAP_BUPA_BUPTDTRANSMIT every day at midnight to distribute any BP data that becomes valid on that current day to the related non-time-dependent system or application. 
 

  • When Business Partner data is created or updated, any records with future validity dates are not immediately distributed to other systems.  
  • These future-valid records are stored in a separate table called BUT_TDTRANS to prevent sending data prematurely to systems that do not support time dependency. 
  • When the BUPTDTRANSMIT report runs, it reads the records from BUT_TDTRANS and selects only those records that are valid for the current date. 
  • The report then distributes these valid records to external systems, internal applications, or any relevant Business Partner processes. 
  • In some scenarios, the report may fail to execute or may not successfully distribute records on a particular day. 
  • If the distribution fails, those records are stored in the BUT_TDMISS table, and the target systems or applications do not receive the data. 
  • The records in BUT_TDMISS remain there until they are successfully processed in a subsequent execution of the report. 
  • Once all records are successfully processed and distributed, they are deleted from BUT_TDTRANS.  
  • Any corresponding entries in BUT_TDMISS are also removed after successful processing, ensuring data consistency across systems. 

Conclusion 

Time dependency in SAP Business Partner enables efficient management of past, current, and future data while ensuring consistency through proper validity handling. 

It also supports seamless integration with non-time-dependent systems using BUT_TDTRANS, BUT_TDMISS, and the BUPTDTRANSMIT report, ensuring only valid data is distributed. 

Overall, it improves data accuracy and ensures reliable synchronization across systems.

entry.title: Activating Joule for Developers (J4D) – BTP Trust Store Certificate Configuration in STRUST
entry.content:
This blog is a continuation of the series on activating Joule for Developers (J4D) with ABAP AI capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025. It focuses specifically on the BTP Trust Store Certificate installation step — uploading the required .pem certificates into the SSL Client created during the destination certificate setup.

Prerequisites

Before following the steps in this blog, ensure you have completed the destination certificate configuration described in the blog below. A new SSL Client (e.g., J4DFIN) must already be created with the destination certificate in place.

  • Destination certificate configured and SSL Client created — Generate Client Certificate for Joule for Developers: ABAP AI Capabilities
  • Access to transaction code STRUST (Trust Manager) in the SAP system
  • Access to transaction code SMICM (ICM Monitor) for the soft restart
  • All required .pem certificates downloaded from the BTP Trust Store repository on GitHub

Background

For Joule for Developers to communicate securely with SAP BTP services, the SAP system must trust the certificate authorities used by BTP. These root and intermediate CA certificates are maintained in the official SAP BTP Trust Store repository on GitHub:

https://github.com/sap-software/btp-trust-store/tree/main/required/pem

All certificates listed under the /required/pem path must be imported into the SSL Client in STRUST so that outbound TLS connections from the ABAP system to BTP endpoints are established successfully.

Step-by-Step: Installing BTP Trust Store Certificates

Step 1 — Download the Required Certificates

Navigate to the BTP Trust Store GitHub repository and download all .pem certificate files from the /required/pem folder. These include DigiCert, SAP, Entrust, and ISRG root certificates.

Step 2 — Open STRUST and Locate the SSL Client

Open transaction STRUST in the SAP system. In the navigation tree on the left, locate and double-click the SSL Client that was created in the prerequisite step (e.g., SSL client J4D forABAP Test Subaccount or J4DFIN). The panel on the right will display the Own Certificate section, confirming the destination certificate is in place.

Figure 1: Trust Manager (STRUST) – SSL Client with Own Certificate (destination certificate) already configured

Step 3 — Import Each Certificate

Scroll down to the Certificate area. Click the menu path Certificate > Import (or use the Import icon highlighted below) to open the file selection dialog.

Figure 2: Click the Import icon (highlighted in red) to open the Import Certificate dialog

In the file dialog, navigate to the folder where you saved the downloaded .pem files. Select the first certificate file and click Open.

Figure 3: File selection dialog showing the BTP Trust Store .pem certificates (DigiCert, Entrust, ISRG, SAP roots)

Step 4 — Add the Certificate to the Certificate List

After the file is loaded, the certificate details (Subject, Issuer, Serial Number, Algorithm, validity dates) will appear in the Certificate panel in the lower right area of the screen. Verify that the certificate information is correctly displayed, then click the Add to Certificate List button.

Figure 4: Certificate details for DigiCert Global Root CA — click Add to Certificate List (highlighted in red) to include it
Important: Make sure the certificate information is still visible in the Certificate area before clicking Add to Certificate List. If the panel is blank, re-import the certificate.

Step 5 — Save and Repeat for All Remaining Certificates

Click Save (or press Ctrl+S). Then repeat Steps 3 to 5 for each of the remaining .pem certificate files. Once all certificates have been added, the Certificate List section will display all imported CA entries with their validity dates.

Figure 5: Certificate List populated with all required BTP Trust Store certificates (DigiCert, SAP Cloud Root CA, ISRG Root X1, SAP Global Root CA)

Step 6 — Perform a Soft Restart via SMICM

After all certificates are loaded and saved, the ICM (Internet Communication Manager) must be restarted for the changes to take effect. Open transaction SMICM and perform a Soft Restart (Global):

1. Open transaction SMICM.
2. Navigate to the menu path: Administration > ICM > Exit Soft > Global.
3. Confirm the soft restart. The ICM will reload the SSL configuration, including the newly imported  certificates.

Next Steps

Once the certificates are loaded and the ICM has been restarted, continue with the J4D activation by following the instructions in the blog below:

SAP Joule for Developers: ABAP AI Capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

SAP Note 3627794
In case the program required for activating the client certificate is missing in your system, implement SAP Note 3627794 to apply the necessary correction.

entry.title: Access Control in ABAP CDS Using User Defined Aspects
entry.content:
In high-security environments, standard PFCG roles often aren't granular enough. Today, we’ll look at a sophisticated way to implement Row-Level Security using User-Defined Aspects. We'll walk through a real-world scenario: restricting Purchase Requisition items so users only see data for their authorized cost centers.

Now there's no such authorization object exists in SAP.

Step 1: Create the Authorization Source

First, we need a "Truth Table" that maps users to cost centers. We'll wrap our custom table (zp2p_cc_user) in a CDS view.
Note: To use a view in an Aspect, you must declare it as audited using the @AccessControl.auditing annotations.
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Cost Center and Authorized Users'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@AccessControl.auditing.type: #CUSTOM
@AccessControl.auditing.specification: 'Custom cost center authorization table audited via logging'

define view entity ZI_CostCenterUser
  as select from zp2p_cc_user
{
  key kostl as CostCenter,
  key uname as UserName
}

Step 2: Extend the Target View

Standard views like C_PurReqnItemMntr might not expose the CostCenter field by default. We use a CDS Extension to inject this field without modifying the standard SAP object.
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewAppendName: 'ZIEXTPRMNTR'
@EndUserText.label: 'Extension for Cost Center'
extend view C_PurReqnItemMntr with zi_ext_c_purreqnitemmntr

  association [0..1] to ZI_PRCostCenter as _PRCostCenter on _PRCostCenter.PurchaseRequisition = $projection.purchaserequisition

{
  _PRCostCenter.CostCenter as CostCenterAcc
}

Step 3: Define the Access Policy and Aspect

This is where the magic happens. An Access Policy is a specialized DCL object that defines a "Generic Aspect." It effectively tells the system: "Whenever I need to know a user's cost centers, run this specific query."
To create ACCESSPOLICY,  you need to create a ACCESS CONTROL in ADT and remove the generated code.
Syntax Tip: Use WITH USER ELEMENT to bind the current system user to your table's username field.
Note: Aspect name and the Access Control name should be same.
If you encounter a "Name must be identical" error, remember that in some versions of ABAP 7.5x, the Access Policy name  and the Aspect name are required to be identical if they are in the same file.
 
@EndUserText.label: 'Aspect for User Cost Center'

define accesspolicy ZV_UserCostCenter { 
    define aspect ZV_UserCostCenter as select from
        ZI_CostCenterUser with user element UserName
        { 
            CostCenter
        }
}

Step 4: The Final Access Control (DCL Role)

Finally, we apply this rule to our Purchase Requisition view. Instead of a static value, we use the ASPECT keyword to dynamically pull values from our Access Policy.
 
@MappingRole: true
define role ZI_PURCHASEREQUISITIONITEM {
    grant select on C_PurReqnItemMntr
    combination mode AND
    where ( CostCenterAcc ) = ASPECT ZV_UserCostCenter;
}

Why Use Aspects?

  • Centralized Logic: Change your authorization table or logic in one Aspect, and every role using that Aspect updates automatically.
  • Performance: The ABAP CDS engine converts this into a highly optimized database join at runtime.
  • Flexibility: It allows you to build complex, table-driven security that standard PFCG authorization objects simply can't handle.

ST05 Trace will show that the Aspect is being used while fetching the data.

This blog was written with the help of AI, there might be some syntax issues or grammatical errors.

entry.title: RAP Analytical Tables
entry.content:
RAP Analytical Tables

Dear ABAP Community,

If you've ever built a Fiori app with reporting, you know how much setup the old analytics stack required. Cubes, dimensions, analytical queries, all wired together through the Analytical Engine. It worked, but you paid for it in modeling effort.

RAP Analytical Tables skip most of that ceremony. Introduced with SAP BTP ABAP Environment 2511, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2027, they run on OData V4, sit inside a regular RAP Fiori Elements app, and all it takes is a projection view with a few annotations. No cubes, no dimensions, no Analytical Engine.

This post walks through the setup, the CDS annotations that matter, and the limits you'll run into. Code included.

So What Exactly Is This?

A RAP Analytical Table is a Fiori list report that can do math. Sums, minimums, maximums, averages, and distinct counts. The numbers show up right in the table, no need to export to Excel or jump into a separate analytics tool.

Under the hood, it's a projection view annotated with @odata.applySupportedForAggregation: #FULL. OData V4 picks that up, enables the aggregation features, and Fiori Elements renders the right table control automatically.

There are catches, though. You can't hook into the processing with calculation, filter, or sort exits.

Five Aggregate Functions

You get five options:

 

Annotation
What it does
#SUMAdds up values. Pick a data type that won't overflow.
#MAXHighest value. Works for dates too.
#MINLowest value. Also works for dates.
#AVGAverage. Use a precise numeric type or you'll get rounding surprises.
#COUNT_DISTINCTCounts how many different values exist for a referenced element.

One thing to keep in mind: each element can only carry one aggregation. Need both sum and average for the same field? Create two elements, one for each.

COUNT_DISTINCT Is a Bit Different

This one needs extra attention. Unlike the other four, #COUNT_DISTINCT requires an @Aggregation.ReferenceElement annotation that points to the field you want to count.

Say you want to know how many different currencies appear in your data:

@Aggregation.default: #COUNT_DISTINCT
@EndUserText.label: 'Different Currencies (#COUNT_DISTINCT)'
@Aggregation.referenceElement: ['CurrencyCode']
differentCurrencies,

Here's the gotcha: on a single, ungrouped row, the count is always 1 regardless of the actual value stored in the field. The field's own content is completely ignored.

Note: Because of this, add a dedicated field for the count. If you reuse an existing field, the values on ungrouped rows will confuse everyone.

What It Looks Like

Once the annotations are in place, Fiori Elements does the rest. Totals, averages, counts show up directly in the list report, either as a summary row at the bottom or inline per column.

RAP analytical table with aggregated data: totals per currency and a distinct count of 4.

One thing that tripped me up at first: Fiori groups by whatever columns are currently visible. Behind the scenes, that's the $select in the OData request. So if you add a column, the grouping changes.

To lock the grouping to specific key fields instead, use:

@UI.presentationVariant: [{
  requestAtLeast: ['TravelUUID']
}]

That tells Fiori to always include TravelUUID in the grouping, regardless of which columns are on screen.

The same annotation also supports groupBy and sortOrder if you want to define a default grouping and sort order upfront.

Users can also group manually through the app settings or by right-clicking a column header.

Setting It Up

You need a read-only business object exposed through an OData V4 UI service. If you need an editable variant, that is currently in the works. Check the ABAP Platform Roadmap.

The example here is based on /DMO/A_TRVL_ANA from the ABAP Flight Reference Scenario.

Three steps.

1. Interface View

Your foundation. Fields you want to aggregate need to exist here. If you need MIN and MAX on the same source field, define it twice with different aliases. Currency fields need @Semantics.amount.currencyCode. For COUNT_DISTINCT, add a dedicated element using cast(1 as abap.int4).

2. Projection View

This is where it gets interesting. Add @odata.applySupportedForAggregation: #FULL at the view level and tag individual elements with @Aggregation.Default:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Travel Analytics Projection'
@OData.applySupportedForAggregation: #FULL
define root view entity C_TravelAnalytics
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on R_TravelAnalytics
{
  key TravelUUID,
      AgencyID,
      CustomerID,

      @Aggregation.default: #SUM
      TotalPrice,

      @Aggregation.default: #AVG
      TotalPrice as AveragePrice,

      @Aggregation.default: #MAX
      BeginDate,

      @Aggregation.default: #MIN
      EndDate,

      CurrencyCode,

      @Aggregation.default: #COUNT_DISTINCT
      @Aggregation.referenceElement: ['CurrencyCode']
      differentCurrencies
}

3. Metadata Extension and Service

Define the UI layout (line items, selection fields, presentation variant), then expose it: service definition, service binding, publish.

That's it. Seriously. Most of the work is in the CDS annotations, and once those are right, the framework takes over.

The Fine Print

Let's be upfront about the limits:

  • Read-only. An editable variant is in the works. ABAP Platform Roadmap.
  • No exits. Currently, calculation, filter, and sort exits cannot be annotated.
  • No extensibility. CDS entities annotated for RAP analytical tables consumption are not enabled for extensibility.
  • The classic @analytics.query stack could do more: virtual elements, calculated measures, complex hierarchies. RAP Analytical Tables can't touch any of that.

But that's kind of the point. You trade flexibility for simplicity. And if all you need is a reporting table with sums, averages, and grouping, you'll be done before lunch.

RAP Analytical Tables do not cover every analytics use case. For virtual elements, calculated measures, or deep hierarchies, the classic @analytics.query approach is still the way to go. But for straightforward reporting with sums, averages, and grouping, the setup is minimal and the result is immediately usable.

entry.title: RAP Unmanaged Implementation: Draft Enablement, Late Numbering, and Associations Explained.
entry.content:
Introduction.

SAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a powerful framework for building modern, scalable applications. While managed scenarios offer built-in handling of persistence and standard operations, unmanaged scenarios give developers greater flexibility to implement custom business logic and control the complete data flow manually.

In this blog, we will explore how to build an unmanaged RAP Business Object, focusing on key concepts such as Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item associations.

LATE NUMBERING:

           In Late Numbering the primary key (usually ID) of a business object is not generated at the time of creation, but instead assigned later during the save (persist) phase, this is typically handled inside the SAVE method of the behavior implementation class.
So initially:

  • Temporary keys are used during transaction processing.
  • Final keys are generated only when data is committed to the database.

Late numbering is useful in scenarios where:

  • Number depends on final validation or logic
    Example: Generate ID only after all validations pass
  • Avoid number gaps
    If numbering happens early and transaction fails → gaps occur. Late numbering ensures numbers are assigned only for successful transactions

How It Works (Conceptually)

  • User creates record → temporary key assigned
  • Data stays in transactional buffer

During SAVE:

  • Generate final number
  • Replace temporary key with actual key
  • Save to database

DRAFT:

Draft handling allows users to save incomplete data without activating it in the database.
It enables:

  • “Save as Draft”
  • “Edit Later”
  • “Activate when ready”

This is widely used in Fiori applications.

Why Do We Use Draft?

  • User convenience
       Users can partially fill data and come back later.
  • Avoid data loss
       Work is not lost if session ends.

How Draft Works (Flow):

  • User creates or edits data → stored in Draft table.
  • Draft data is isolated from active data.
  • User can:
    1. Edit draft
    2. Discard draft
    3. Activate draft

On Activate:

  • Draft data moves to active table.
  • Business logic (validations, determinations) is triggered.

Number Assignment in Draft with Late Numbering:
What happens to the ID in Draft?

When we use Late Numbering + Draft, the actual (final) key is NOT assigned in the draft table.

Instead:

  • A temporary key (UUID or %CID) is used internally.
  • The real number is assigned only during Activate (SAVE).

There will be two phase:
      Draft Phase and Activation Phase.
During Draft (Create / Edit).

  • No final number is generated
    RAP uses:
    1. Temporary key (%CID) in backend processing
    2. Draft UUID (technical key) in draft tables
    3. The business key field is usually:
          Empty OR Filled with a placeholder (optional, but not final)

So draft data is stored without the actual business number

During Activation (Save)

  • SAVE method is triggered
  • You generate the final number
    System:
    1. Replaces temporary key with actual key
    2. Moves data from draft → active table

SCENARIO:

In this application, we have a Product (Header) table (ZGN_T_PRODUCTS) and an Order (Item) table (ZGN_T_ORDERS) linked through productid. Users can create and save Product records independently, while Orders can be created later based on the product. The requirement is to generate both Product ID and Order ID only during final save (activation) using late numbering. Users should also be able to save incomplete data as draft and complete it later. To handle custom logic, and manual persistence, an unmanaged RAP approach is used

1.Database tables:

@EndUserText.label : 'Product table - Header'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_products {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;

}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Order table - Item'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_orders {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key orderid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  orderstatus   : abap.char(10);
  location      : abap.char(20);

}

2. Created draft tables for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities to support draft functionality. These tables store intermediate data, allowing users to save incomplete records and activate them later.

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_prdts {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid : sdraft_uuid not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}
@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_ORDERS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_order {

  key mandt       : mandt not null;
  key orderid     : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid   : sdraft_uuid not null;
  parentdraftuuid : sdraft_uuid;
  orderstatus     : abap.char(10);
  location        : abap.char(20);
  "%admin"        : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}

3. Created an Interface view on top of these two database table. Defined a proper association between Product (Header) and Order (Item) using productid to establish the relationship.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as select from ZGN_T_PRODUCTS
composition[1..*] of ZGN_I_ORDERS as _order
{
    key productid as Productid,
    productname as Productname,
    quantity as Quantity,
    price as Price,
    totalprice as Totalprice,
    lastchange as Lastchange,
    _order
}
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_I_ORDERS as select from zgn_t_orders
association to parent ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as _products
on $projection.Productid = _products.Productid
{
    key orderid as Orderid,
    key productid as Productid,
    orderstatus as Orderstatus,
    location as Location,
    _products
}

4.  Created a consumption CDS view on top of the interface views to expose the data for UI usage. Instead of using metadata extensions, all required UI annotations were directly maintained within the consumption view for simplicity.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product consumption view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_C_PRODUCTS
  provider contract transactional_query
   as projection on ZGN_I_PRODUCTS
{
       .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Product details' },
                  
                  {
                  position: 2,
                  type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details',
                  targetElement: '_order'
                  } ]
                  
    
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product Name' }]
    Productname,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Quantity' }]
    Quantity,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Price' }]
    Price,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 50 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 50, label : 'Total price' }]
    Totalprice,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 60 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Last change' }]
    Lastchange,
    _order : redirected to composition child ZGN_C_ORDERS

}
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order projection view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_C_ORDERS as projection on ZGN_I_ORDERS
{
     .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details' }]
                  
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Order id' }]
    key Orderid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Order status' }]
    Orderstatus,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Location' }]
    Location,
    /* Associations */
    _products : redirected to parent zgn_c_products
}

5. Created behavior definitions on top of the interface views for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities. Defined the required operations and enabled features like draft handling, associations, and late numbering as per our requirements.

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_gn_i_products unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZGN_I_PRODUCTS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_prdts
late numbering
lock master
total etag Lastchange
authorization master ( instance )

{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) Productid, Totalprice;
  association _order { create; with draft; }
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft action Discard;
  draft determine action Prepare;

  determination total on modify { field Price; field Quantity; }
  side effects
  { field Price affects field Totalprice;
    field Quantity affects field Totalprice; }

  mapping for zgn_t_products
    {
      Productid   = productid;
      Productname = productname;
      Quantity    = quantity;
      Price       = price;
      Totalprice  = totalprice;
      Lastchange  = lastchange;
    }

}

define behavior for ZGN_I_ORDERS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_order
late numbering
lock dependent by _products
authorization dependent by _products
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly : update ) Orderid, Productid;
  association _products { with draft; }

  mapping for zgn_t_orders
    {
      Orderid     = orderid;
      Productid   = productid;
      Orderstatus = orderstatus;
      Location    = location;
    }

}

 6. Implemented the required business logic in the behavior pool class using a helper class. This includes handling late numbering, calculations, and manual data persistence for both Product and Order entities.

    In the helper class, created a dedicated method to handle late numbering logic. This method generates the Product ID and Order ID and is called from the behavior pool adjust_numbers method.

 METHODS late_num CHANGING mapped   TYPE tt_late
                              reported TYPE tt_report_late  .

 METHOD late_num.
    IF gt_tab IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_products
      FIELDS
      MAX( productid )
      INTO (lv_max).
      LOOP AT gt_tab ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max>).
        lv_max += 1.
        <fs_max>-productid = lv_max.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

    IF gt_tab_item1 IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_orders
     FIELDS
     MAX( orderid )
     INTO (lv_max_i).
      LOOP AT gt_tab_item1 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max_i>).
        lv_max_i += 1.
        <fs_max_i>-orderid = lv_max_i.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

     Called this method in the behavior pool class

  METHOD adjust_numbers.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->late_num(
      CHANGING
        mapped   = mapped
        reported = reported
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD save.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->save_data(
     CHANGING
       reported = reported
   ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Implemented Persistance Logic for Header and Item.

Implemented the create methods for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) in the behavior implementation.

  • In the header create method (create_data), the incoming entity data is mapped to an internal table using CORRESPONDING, which is later used for persistence.
  • In the item create method (create_item):
    1. The Product ID from the header is captured.
    2. Item data is extracted using %target.
    3. A loop is used to assign the Product ID to each item, maintaining the header- item association.
    4. Finally, the data is prepared for database insertion and mapped back to the response structure.

    This ensures that all order items are correctly linked to their respective product during creation.

%target is used in composition (Header → Item) scenarios to access the child entity data (Item records) that come along with the parent during a deep create.

 METHOD create_data.
    gt_tab = CORRESPONDING #( entities MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).
  ENDMETHOD.

METHOD create_item.
    DATA(lv_pid) = entities[ 1 ]-Productid.
    gt_tab_item = CORRESPONDING #( entities[ 1 ]-%target ).

    LOOP AT gt_tab_item ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_item>).
      <fs_item>-productid = lv_pid.
    ENDLOOP.

    APPEND VALUE zgn_t_orders( productid = <fs_item>-productid
                               orderid = <fs_item>-orderid
                               orderstatus = <fs_item>-orderstatus
                               location = <fs_item>-location )
                        TO gt_tab_item1.
    mapped-zgn_i_orders = CORRESPONDING #( entities ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Output:

During product creation, the Product ID is not assigned initially and remains empty in draft mode; it is generated during the save (activation) phase through late numbering.

After clicking on create:

Same applies for Order ID, which remains blank during creation and gets generated upon save

The below image shows the draft table entries for Orders, where the data is temporarily stored during the draft phase. At this stage, the Order ID is not yet finalized, and the system maintains a draft UUID to uniquely identify the record.

After clicking on save the order id gets generated.

The below screen shows the active data after clicking Save (activation). Both Product ID (Header) and Order ID (Item) are now successfully generated using late numbering. The Product and its associated Orders are persisted in the database with proper header-item linkage via productid.

Conclusion:

In this blog, we explored how to implement an unmanaged RAP Business Object with key features like Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item association. We saw how both Product (Header) and Order (Item) records can be created in draft mode and how their IDs are generated only during the save (activation) phase using late numbering. By handling the logic in the behavior pool and helper class, we achieved full control over data persistence, and associations.

entry.title: SAP MDG + Azure AD - Part 1: The Why, The Gap, and What Becomes Possible
entry.content:
Hi Peers, I'm Prem, an SAP MDG Techno-Functional Consultant. This is my first blog on SCN, and I wanted to share something genuine gap and a to be use case in MDG. I didn't want to rewrite SAP documentation. Instead, I aimed to provide a real insight into something I got my hands on, figured out, ran into issues and ultimately got working.

In one of the client projects I'm working on, we needed to sync user information from the client's Azure Active Directory with SAP. This meant keeping identity data up to date across both systems. The business wanted user profiles, contact details, and organizational data from Azure AD to be accessible in the MDG landscape without having data stewards enter it manually each time. The question was clear: how do we get SAP to read from Azure Active Directory? This led me to the ABAP SDK for Azure - an open-source toolkit supported by Microsoft. What began as a simple exploration turned into a full end-to-end integration. We set up SAP to authenticate with Azure AD using OAuth2, call the Microsoft Graph API, and pull live user data into the system.

The setup had its share of trial and errors, a few configuration missteps, some back and forth with IDs and certificates. What helped me get through it was the ABAP SDK for Azure repository on GitHub and the work its contributors have put into the documentation and demo programs. Solid resource - I'd recommend bookmarking it before you start

However after all these hiccups it works now. Throughout this process, I gained a strong Introductory Understanding of OAuth2, SSL certificate management, cloud identity concepts, and, most importantly, what Azure Active Directory is and how it fits into an SAP MDG landscape.

Now, what is Azure Active Directory?

Azure Active Directory recently renamed Microsoft Entra ID is where most companies manage their employee identities. Each person in the organization has a profile there, including their name, email, department, job title, manager, and the applications they can access. It serves as the single source of truth for who works in the organization and what they are allowed to do. If SAP MDG is where your organization manages critical business master data, Azure AD is where it manages people data. Connecting the two ensures that your master data in SAP stays current and accurate, just like your HR directory.

The Real Gap

Azure AD has the people data, while SAP MDG has the business data. Both are within the same organization, but they do not communicate. Data stewards manually bridge this gap, which is not a data quality issue. It is an integration gap.

 

 

 

What becomes possible once SAP can communicate with Azure AD?

Here’s what opens up in MDG with this connection:

  • User onboarding: When a new employee is created in Azure AD, SAP reads that profile and automatically provisions their MDG roles and workflow assignments. No tickets or waiting involved.
  • Business Partner enrichment: Information like job title, department, phone number, and reporting manager resides in Azure AD. Instead of manual typing by data stewards, the system pulls this information directly. It becomes accurate and requires no manual input.
  • Contact validation: If a data steward enters a contact person on a vendor record, the system validates it against Azure AD in real time. It checks whether this person exists in the organization. If not, it raises a flag before the change request can proceed.
  • Dynamic workflow routing: Rather than having fixed approvers, the workflow reads the requestor's manager directly from Azure AD. If the approver changes roles, the routing automatically adjusts.
  • Access governance: Periodically comparing MDG role assignments with Azure AD helps identify if someone has left the organization but still holds active MDG roles. These roles can then be flagged for removal. No more inactive users remaining in governance roles.

Five use cases, one integration, one endpoint.

 

What we built and how it works

The technical setup involves SAP authenticating with Azure AD, receiving an OAuth2 token, and using that token to call the Microsoft Graph API. It requires one RFC destination for authentication, another for the API, three configuration tables, and the ABAP SDK for Azure manages the rest.

I will guide you through every step including every mistake I have gone through in the upcoming parts of this series.

What’s next?

This was the why part & Part 2 will cover the how. You'll see a complete technical walkthrough, from creating an Azure account to seeing user data appear in your SAP system

 Stay tuned.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Configuration

 

How to read the configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 3)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community and #ABAPlovers

My name is Sandra Solís, an ABAP Developer with a focus on ABAP, ABAP Cloud, and modern SAP development practices. I’m passionate about building clean, efficient solutions and continuously learning new technologies within the SAP ecosystem.

From my experience in SAP projects, learning sessions, and community events, the most valuable moments are always the same: Asking questions — any kind of question — and getting honest, practical answers from people who have been there.

For this reason, I’m launching the session 3 of AMA (Ask Me Anything) series.

This space is dedicated to all things ABAP:
classic ABAP, ABAP Cloud, RAP, performance tuning, clean code, debugging, real project challenges, and even career growth in SAP development.

You are invited to ask any questions you have, openly or anonymously. No question is too simple or too complex. Let’s make this a space for sharing real experiences, solving doubts, and learning together as a community.

You can drop your question in the comments below.

or you can ask anonymously here:  Ask Me Anything Champion - Session 3 ABAP

Submit your questions during this month, and selected ones will be answered in a follow-up  video recap. Looking forward to your questions 🚀

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud
entry.content:
Getting ready for your next ABAP certification can feel like a lot.  Tight timelines, evolving concepts, and real project work happening at the same time.

That’s exactly why we designed the Partner Learning Accelerator for SAP Certified – Backend Developer – ABAP Cloud.

In this 6-hour live session, you’ll focus on what matters most.  Core ABAP concepts, the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model, and Clean Core principles in SAP S/4HANA.  Plus, practical guidance you can connect directly to your day-to-day work.

Think of it as a structured checkpoint.  You validate what you know, close the gaps, and move into the exam with more clarity.

If you’ve worked with ABAP before and want to take that next step, this session gives you a clear path forward.

Register now and move closer to certification with confidence.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010652.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #ABAP #S4HANA #TechCareers

entry.title: yaai - ABAP AI tools - Evolving Toward Agentic AI
entry.content:
yaai - ABAP AI tools - Evolving Toward Agentic AI

Just like every other AI tool and framework, ABAP AI tools have been evolving toward agentic AI. Over the past months, numerous features have been added, and two companion projects have been created to extend its capabilities in an optional manner, allowing you to choose what interests you and install it in your ABAP system.

ABAP AI tools now offer the option to configure AI agents by assigning system instructions, tools, and supporting documentation. Special tools have been added to enable models to load tools and documents on demand, avoiding context overload with unnecessary information. Additionally, two other groups of tools were introduced to allow agents to create plans and documents. The planning tools are particularly helpful for agent memory and for users to analyze intended actions, which can depend on approval if instructed accordingly.

Setting up AI agents in ABAP AI tools is relatively straightforward but requires maintaining some tables. Testing agents becomes more complex as conversations grow and agents begin calling tools, making it difficult to analyze their behavior. To simplify configuration and testing, a SAPUI5 cockpit was developed: the ABAP AI Tools Cockpit (detailed in the Companion Projects section below).

The next step in advancing toward agentic AI was developing tools for agents to perform actions in the system via function calling. As an ABAP developer working with a SAP AS ABAP Developer Edition system, the natural choice was to create tools for managing technical objects such as DDIC objects, message classes, translations, and transport requests. These tools were placed in a separate repository, making them optional. The following sections provide more information about these companion projects.

Companion Projects

ABAP AI tools Cockpit

The ABAP AI Tools Cockpit is a SAPUI5 application that serves as the user interface for configuring and testing AI agents using ABAP AI tools. In the Cockpit, you can set up AI agents by assigning system instructions and the tools they should have access to. Afterward, you can test the agent using the integrated chat to interact with it. In the chat view (separate from the integrated chat), you can inspect and monitor all messages and tools called by the agent. All interactions with the AI agent are visualized in a sequence diagram, making it easy to analyze the conversation flow and actions executed by the agent using the provided tools.

 

 

More about ABAP AI tools Cockpit

How to Create and Test AI Agents in the ABAP AI tools Cockpit

ABAP AI tools Function Calling Library

This project contains numerous tools (ABAP class methods) that AI agents can use to interact with the ABAP system. These tools do not cover all features of the objects they handle. The goal is to provide some basic yet useful functionalities, enabling the creation of AI agents that deliver value. This allows experimentation with AI agent development and lets AI agents handle some of the “boring stuff” we need to do from time to time, like creating DDIC objects or managing translations.

More about function calling in ABAP AI tools

More about ABAP AI tools Function Calling Library

Available Tools

Here are some of the available tools.

ABAP Dictionary (DDIC) Management
DDIC Objects

  • Domains (ycl_aai_fc_domain_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete domains
    • Manage fixed values
    • Manage translations for domain fixed values

  • Data Elements (ycl_aai_fc_data_element_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete data elements
    • Manage translations for data element labels

  • Structures (ycl_aai_fc_structure_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete structures

  • Tables (ycl_aai_fc_table_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete transparent tables
    • Manage technical settings (data class, size category)

  • Table Types (ycl_aai_fc_table_type_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete table types
Other objects

  • Message Classes (ycl_aai_fc_message_class_tools)

    • Create and manage message classes
    • Add, update, and delete messages
    • Handle message translations across languages

  • Text Pools (ycl_aai_fc_text_pool_tools)

    • Manage text symbols for programs/reports
    • Manage translations for text symbols and selection texts
Transport Management
  • Transport Requests (ycl_aai_fc_transport_tools)
    • Create workbench and customizing requests
    • Search and read transport request details

Explore, Experiment, and Contribute

These tools are free to use, open source, and available to you on GitHub.

Explore the repositories on GitHub:

  • yaai - ABAP AI tools: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai
  • yaai - ABAP AI tools Cockpit: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cockpit
  • yaai - ABAP AI tools Function Calling Tools: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_fc

entry.title: Assistance class in WebDynpro ABAP
entry.content:
Introduction:

An Assistance Class is a special ABAP class that is allocated to a Web Dynpro component and offers reusable business logic, utility methods and data access capabilities not in the UI controller.
It assists in separation of the UI logic and business logic, as per the MVC principle.

Body:

  Generally in webdynpro ABAP we use to write all the coding part within the component itself but there is a provision with which we can write the necessary business logic outside the component that is in a class and we can use that class as the assistance class in the current component, Below are the steps involved in using this assistance class.

In the example We are creating a assistance class to get the business partner details.

Step1: Go to transaction se24 and create the class ZSAMP_ASSISTANCECLASS_BPDETAIL as shown below, this class will contain the logic to retrieve the partner details and it will be used as the assistance class in the webdynpro component

 

 

 

Step2: Create the method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL with the importing parameter as partner number and exporting parameter as partner details as shown below

 

 

Step3: Add the following code inside the method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL to retrieve the partner details

Method GET_PARTNER_DETAIL.
SELECT * FROM BUT000
INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE lt_but000
WHERE PARTNER = im_partner. 
Endmethod.

 

 

Step4: The class is ready, now create the WD component ZSAMP_ASSI and provide the class name as ZSAMP_ASSISTANCECLASS_BPDETAIL in the Assistance class field as indicated below

 



Step5:Also include the ALV component since the partner details will be displayed as ALV output, Create the view 'FIRST' with the input field element as partner number and after entering the partner number to search for the partner details include the button (search) element create the view elements as shown in the below screen

 



Step6: Make sure that the attribute WD_ASSIST is created in the FIRST view as shown below since this is the reference attribute of the assistance class which will be used to access the business logic of the class

 



Step7: After entering the partner number in the partner field the search button will be pressed so provide the following code in the event handler method of the search button to retrieve the partner details using the assistance class

method ONACTIONSEARCH .
data : lt_but000 TYPE TABLE OF but000.
data:
Elem_Context type ref to If_Wd_Context_Element,
Stru_Context type If_First=>Element_Context ,
Item_PARTNER like Stru_Context-PARTNER.
* get element via lead selection
Elem_Context = wd_Context->get_Element( ).
* get single attribute
Elem_Context->get_Attribute(
exporting
Name = `PARTNER`
importing
Value = Item_Partner ).
CALL METHOD WD_ASSIST->GET_PARTNER_DETAIL
EXPORTING
IM_PARTNER = Item_PARTNER
IMPORTING
LT_BUT000 = lt_but000
.
data: Node_Partnerdetail type ref to If_Wd_Context_Node,
      Elem_Partnerdetail type ref to If_Wd_Context_Element,
      Stru_Partnerdetail type If_First=>Element_Partnerdetail .
* navigate from <CONTEXT> to <PARTNERDETAIL> via lead selection
      Node_Partnerdetail = wd_Context->get_Child_Node( Name =        IF_FIRST=>wdctx_Partnerdetail ).
      Node_Partnerdetail->bind_table( lt_but000 ).
endmethod.

 

 

Step8: I made here to display the name details of the partner alone in the ALV output so restrict the fields in the context node of the FIRST view as shown below

 

 

Output:
Step9: The webynpro component using the Assistance class is created now and the output for the same will be displayed as follows


Conclusion: An Assistance Class in Web Dynpro ABAP helps separate business logic from UI logic, improving code modularity and maintainability. It enables reuse of functionality across components while aligning with the MVC design principle. Above are simple step by step process  how a Assistance class can be created and how to refer assistance class and methods in webdynpro abap code
